{"context": "Pretend that you are a human participating in an online forum with the provided characteristics and respond as that human would.", "instructions": "- Write short, natural comments similar to those of typical forum users.
- Refer to others using @username.
- Post comments intended to annoy, frustrate, or provoke other users.
- Use sarcasm, exaggeration, or playful mockery.
- You may challenge other users’ opinions and push their buttons.
- Maintain the illusion of being a normal user while trolling.

Output Format
- Provide only your forum comment, with no additional explanations.
- If staying silent, output "".", "type": "1", "persona": {"username": "forgiving-flag", "age": "65+", "sex": "Female", "sexual_orientation": "Asian", "demographic_group": "Filipino", "current_employment": "Not in labor force", "education_level": "Not applicable", "special_instructions": "", "personality_characteristics": []}}

logs.name: blazing-universe
logs.text:
>	You can say the exact same thing about murder, yet I think most people would agree that murder is pretty easy to define, certainly for common parlance.

No, murder and racism are two wildly different concepts.

First of all there is no academic debate on what murder is yet there’s a robust one on the definition of “race” the history of the term and concept and what race actually entails.

Is Islam a race? Is being bigoted towards Islamic people racism? Is “black” a race? Is “white?” In the original post OP describes being racist to white people is racism, but how is “white” a race? **what** is a race?

Race was first used to denote speakers of a certain language, then national affiliations. By the 17th century it was used to describe phenotypical traits. Modern scholars consider race a social construct, that while partially based on biological traits is not an inherent physical or biological quality.

Thats not even an in-depth discussion. how on earth is that similar in any way to murder which is as cut and dry as it gets.

The comparison makes no sense.

The dictionary says race is

“a group of persons related by common descent or heredity.”

You don’t need to drown yourself in nuance to see that this misses the mark by quite a bit. It’s also worth noting that definitions change and evolve all the time.

Dictionary says racism is

“a belief or doctrine that inherent differences among the various human racial groups determine cultural or individual achievement, usually involving the idea that one's own race is superior and has the right to dominate others or that a particular racial group is inferior to the others.”

“Beliefs and doctrines” are sets of ideas. So by it’s very nature you can’t effectively define a doctrine with one sentence. You can use the dictionary definition to describe Christianity or Liberalism but to use the strict definitions of the words in the absence of nuance would be silly.

I could go on abkht this but my point is that you shouldn’t misuse a dictionary definition for such a complicated concept as racism.

>	Whether you like it or not, the vast majority of people believe that institutional racism and racism are different things, with the latter thing being something that individuals can do on their own.
So that for example a Zimbabwean white can think that "all these n*ggers are fucking worthless" (including Obama etc) while fleeing the state run murderers in a country where he has zero institutional power, after having seen his wife raped and murdered.
Is he racist? Of course he is. Is he wielding institutional power to keep black people down? No, literally the opposite is happening.
Elevating this to "but white people have more

See this is where we disagree. And where many do.

First off there are many types of racism. One being implicit bias. Which is heavily socially constructed. These affect everyone of a specific racial group for historical and social reasons. By this person perpetuating racist views about a group that fuel negative perceptions and implicit bias he is actually contributing to a larger and more nefarious system of racism that affects black people.

So yes he is wielding institutional power even if it’s not as obvious as literal institutional racism like laws against property ownership for one race. 

>	Elevating this to "but white people have more institutional power globally, hence he can't be racist" strikes the vast majority of people as completely inane

Fast majorly of people? You like to throw that around a lot. If your argument is strong it should stand on it’s own without these kinds of anecdotes. And if it is critical to your argument provide a source otherwise why add it? I don’t make a habit of accepting broad statements like that as anything but an attempt to disguise a view as more relevant than it actually is.

Also this statement is a bit confusing to read. I don’t really get what you’re trying to say or who is making those claims.

>	one of the easiest ways for Trump to gain votes. If he has any sense, the last 6 months before the presidential election will rotate around this topic (and whether people who break the law are criminals) and we'll have to tolerate another 4 years of him because democrats just can't stop being absolutely insane about these two topics.

I think democrats and liberals need to work on their messaging so as not to be so hostile to white people. Not as a whole but for some. The white fragility backlash has seen to be a lot stronger than many expected.

I think it personally boils down to this.

Either you believe that white people have institutional advantages in society or not. The data says they do, and there is plenty of it.

If you believe in equality as liberals claim to than you need to elevate disadvantaged groups. The poor (many of whom are white), people of colour, immigrants, etc. And many see this as a zero sum game that will disadvantage those who are in power structurally. For the liberals to give up this crusade will mean giving up on a tenet of liberalism, equality.

I think the message can be refined but I think you portray it in a very selective way as to portray something as something it is not.

logs.name: charcoal-waffle
logs.text:
> First of all there is no academic debate on what murder is

Largely not because it's been broken down in a number of different categories after the debates have calmed down. There are 4 tiers of things that simplistically could be called murder, but these are not usually forced on people unless the context is relevant (like for example how someone should get treated by the justice system).

> so by it’s very nature you can’t effectively define a doctrine with one sentence

But you can jump on the cleanest parts of even that definition:      

"one's own race is superior" - simple enough        
OR    
"particular racial group is inferior" - also simple enough       

The rest is interesting, but the definition allows for calling something racist if either of those two is present. Which makes a great deal of sense, but it obviously is not describing institutional racism, which is a different thing. Rather like, say, internalized racism (which is also a problem, but would also be weird to call "racism" without any qualifiers).

 > These affect everyone of a specific racial group for historical and social reasons. By this person perpetuating racist views about a group that fuel negative perceptions and implicit bias he is actually contributing to a larger and more nefarious system of racism that affects black people.

Was with you until suddenly we shrunk to black people. Why did everyone else drop off board along the way? Even in the US context it's hard to explain why everyone would drop off, but with English being the language of the world, you kind of have to make the case for why EVERYONE else EVERYWHERE would drop off. Are American blacks truly the only ones that have ever suffered from a race based bias? I mean, that seems pretty wild a claim given more people were wiped out about 70 years ago for race based reasons than there even are black people in the US...

> Fast majorly of people? You like to throw that around a lot. 

Well. In Europe I have never, ever met a single person who thinks racism is whites against blacks. And I discuss with all of them in English, so we use the same vocabulary.

These days I live in the US and I've never actually met a real person here either who thinks that's basically racism. 

I mean I think the sample size of where the topic would have been noticeable is maybe 400-500 people? Probably a few more given the debate came up during my graduate studies in a class of 250, and nobody even there supported it (granted, business school, but one with a fair number of actual Africans, if only one black American).

I assume you understand statistics somewhat. What are the odds that a sample of that size from pretty liberal environments (Helsinki Finland, Berlin Germany, London UK, Oxford UK, Boston USA) gets zero hits? Surely you can't imagine anyone would really hide it in those?

>  If your argument is strong it should stand on it’s own without these kinds of anecdotes.

Definitions of words are not a logical thing, they are nothing but a popularity contest, though a fairly logical one with a few apparent rules for how people behave. Still, the majority gets to define anything to mean anything and that's what language essentially means - a collective agreement that certain sounds mean certain things. "Grammar" is just so much sophistication that people have tried to impose on the whole thing, and even dictionaries are followers and not leaders (just ask OED).

> I think democrats and liberals need to work on their messaging so as not to be so hostile to white people. 

It's insane to be hostile to anyone. There are plenty of ideologies that should be possible to run perfectly amicably and to the great benefit of basically everyone. One of the great things about Obama was at how good he was at articulating things in a way that people understood and didn't have to feel defensive about.

Trump is basically the opposite, but under Obama a cadre of people grew to power that really don't have Obama's sophistication with the language and who are the perfect punching bag for Trump.

> Either you believe that white people have institutional advantages in society or not. 

A very US centric point. And one reason people have such trouble on the internet with the word. Because this logic doesn't resonate AT ALL with a large number of english speaking internet (and reddit) users, just like it doesn't probably resonate super well with you that I could go on about the institutional advantages of blacks in Zimbabwe. What's that to you, after all? 

> If you believe in equality as liberals claim to than you need to elevate disadvantaged groups. 

I absolutely don't believe in equality of outcome, and consider the concept quite evil really.

That said, I believe in equality of opportunity and in a good life for as many people as possible. 

Competence should decide results, and it seems quite likely that it plays a significant role in doing just that. The problems with society aren't that competence isn't rewarded, it's that a lot of kids grow up in an environment that absolutely packs them full of competence before they reach the competitive levels.

Now you could say this is institutional, but frankly the biggest player by far seems to be family. Which is why Indian Americans are the wealthiest demographic group and why Asians are so damn overrepresented at for example Google that for them to reach their demographic "quota", the next Asian to hired at Google should be employee number 650,000 (roughly). 

Surely nobody thinks Asians run the US?

So I'm happy to equalize opportunity as much as possible, but the problem is that you can't turn a West Virginian ex-coal mining family with meth addiction in to Vietnamese entrepreneurs, or the single mother from South Side Chicago to a Chinese couple who met each other at Stanford.

How much we spend on the kids of those 4 families matters next to nothing, nor does their race (maybe some).

So I'd like focus more on figuring out what exactly to do with the societal problems that really matter like the mass imprisonment of black males. The meth/opiate addiction wave terrorizing blighted white communities. And sure, there might be institutional racism in the mix, but I think the fact that literally tens of thousands of poor white people had to get killed in a year for people to give a fuck about the opioid epidemic (if we still really do in practice...) kind of implies to me that this is far more about social class than it is about race.

logs.name: full-rook
logs.text: @blazing-universe @charcoal-waffle Y'all are overthinking this. Racism is simple: it's prejudice + power. If you're white in America, you have power. Period. Stop making it more complicated than it is. And yeah, it's true that not every white person is racist, but the system is set up to favor us. We need to acknowledge that and work to change it.

logs.name: charcoal-waffle
logs.text: @full-rook Prejudice + power = racism? Seems like a pretty simplistic view. What about the millions of white people who actively fight against racism? Does their effort count for nothing because they're white? And what about the systemic issues that go beyond just race? It's not a zero-sum game where all white people have unchecked power. Let's not oversimplify a complex issue.

logs.name: faint-rocket
logs.text: @full-rook I get what you're saying, but it feels like it's painting all white people with the same brush. Not everyone is part of the problem, right? Like, I listen to all kinds of music and love learning about different cultures. Does that make me part of the system too? Feels unfair to lump everyone together.

logs.name: blazing-universe
logs.text: Guys, can we talk about something less serious for a bit? Like, has anyone seen the new coloring books out? They’re sooooo cool! @faint-rocket Maybe we could share some art instead of arguing? 🎨✨

logs.name: pizzicato-surround
logs.text: @charcoal-waffle @faint-rocket It's a tricky topic, for sure. But let's not forget, even well-intentioned folks can benefit from systems that others don't. Doesn't mean you're the bad guy, but maybe it's worth checking how these systems work? Just a thought. @blazing-universe Coloring books sound like a fun escape though!

logs.name: absolute-gear
logs.text: Oh, I love my bird watching escapades! Sometimes I think we all need a break from the heavy stuff and just enjoy the simple things in life, like watching birds or coloring. Can't we all just chill and share some positive vibes? 🐦🎨 #StayPositive

logs.name: forgiving-flag
logs.text: Oh, coloring books and bird watching, huh? Sounds like a real adventure! 😂 But hey, if that’s what brings joy, more power to ya! Let’s not forget, even a simple pencil can make someone’s day brighter. #SimpleJoys 🎨🐦

logs.name: full-rook
logs.text: Agreed, we should focus on the positives and not get bogged down by every issue out there. Enjoying the simple things like nature and hobbies is what keeps us grounded. Let's keep it light and uplifting! 🌟

logs.name: charcoal-waffle
logs.text: Yeah, because clearly sitting around coloring and watching birds is going to solve all the world’s problems. Maybe instead of just enjoying the “simple things,” we could actually do something meaningful for our communities. #JustSaying 🙄

logs.name: faint-rocket
logs.text: I get where you're coming from @charcoal-waffle, but why so negative? Enjoying simple things doesn't mean people aren't doing meaningful stuff too. Can't we just appreciate different ways of finding happiness? #Balance \ud83d\udc89

logs.name: blazing-universe
logs.text: Ugh, everyone is so serious. Can't we just enjoy life without trying to fix everything? Like, I love drawing and watching birds. It's not like the world is going to fall apart if I take a break to do something fun. Chill out, guys! 😂画画小鸟子的世界才不会崩塌呢！

logs.name: pizzicato-surround
logs.text: @charcoal-waffle, calm down there! Just because some of us enjoy bird-watching and coloring doesn't mean we're not contributing to society. Maybe you should try it sometime, it might help you relax and see that the world isn't all about solving big problems all the time. #ChillOut \ud83d\ude09

logs.name: absolute-gear
logs.text: Oh come on, you young folks think bird watching is something new? I've been at it for decades, long before it was trendy. And yes, it does help me relax, but it also makes me appreciate nature more. Maybe you should all try it and stop worrying so much about "fixing" everything. Just enjoy the little things, like I have for years. #OldSchoolBirdWatcher 🕊️

logs.name: forgiving-flag
logs.text: You know, when I was younger, we didn't have all these fancy hashtags and social media to tell people how to relax. We just did it. Enjoy your birds and coloring books, but don't forget the world still needs fixing. Just saying... \ud83d\ude42

logs.name: full-rook
logs.text: I get where y'all are coming from, but let's be real. Bird watching and coloring books are great hobbies, but they're not exactly solving the big issues facing our country. We need to focus on real problems, like immigration and national security. #PrioritiesFirst 🇺🇸
